desc: Asunción, Paraguay's riverfront capital on the Paraguay River, mixes neoclassical government landmarks, relaxed neighborhoods, and one of South America's most underrated food-and-nightlife scenes. Climate is the key planning variable: cooler, drier months from about May to August are easiest for daytime walking, while spring-summer brings heavier heat and storm risk.
hls: Compact historic core around Palacio de López and Costanera
Strong local food scene (sopa paraguaya, chipa, grilled meats)
Good base for day trips to Areguá, San Bernardino, and Itaipú route connections
Lower international visitor density than regional capitals
Affordable hotels and restaurants outside major event weeks
Riverfront sunsets and lively evening social culture

todo.name: Walk central Asunción
todo.desc: Use a half-day orientation walk through the historic/core districts and local markets.

todo.name: Museum and heritage stop
todo.desc: Anchor one cultural visit to understand local history and identity.

todo.name: Local food circuit
todo.desc: Plan a focused evening around regional specialties and neighborhood dining.

todo.name: Scenic viewpoint session
todo.desc: Schedule sunrise or sunset from a top city/nearby viewpoint.

todo.name: Nature or coast day trip
todo.desc: Reserve one full day for nearby nature, coast, or mountain access.

todo.name: Flexible slow day
todo.desc: Keep one low-structure day for weather pivots and spontaneous plans.

wifi.note: Urban hotels/cafés are usually stable; speeds can soften in outer districts or remote day-trip zones.

prac.fltNote: No nonstop UK-Asunción route; common one-stop patterns are via Madrid (MAD), São Paulo-Guarulhos (GRU), or Panama City (PTY) into ASU. South American hub-to-ASU legs can tighten around Christmas/New Year and July school-holiday periods, so protect connections with longer layovers.
prac.currency: Paraguayan guaraní (PYG). Cards are accepted in malls/hotels, but cash is still important for markets, taxis, and neighborhood restaurants.
prac.lang: Spanish and Guaraní are both official and widely used; English availability is limited outside business hotels and higher-end venues.
prac.tz: PYT (UTC-4) with summer daylight-saving shifts to PYST (UTC-3), typically around October-March; confirm exact clock changes for your dates.
prac.visa: Paraguay is visa-exempt for many nationalities (including UK/EU for short tourism), while some passports require pre-arranged visas; verify rules with the Paraguayan consulate/official immigration sources before ticketing.
alerts: From November to March, afternoon heat index can exceed safe sightseeing thresholds; schedule outdoor walks early and keep electrolyte/water backups.
Intense thunderstorms can flood low-lying streets quickly in summer; avoid tight airport transfers when severe-weather alerts are active.
Cross-border shopping/day trips (Argentina or Brazil routes) can face long immigration queues on holiday weekends, so carry printed booking proof and allow generous buffer time.

sweet: May-August is usually the comfort sweet spot for city exploration; September-October can also work well before peak heat and storms build.
